title: Binomial Probability Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate P(X=k) for binomial distributions. Find probability, mean, and variance for n trials with probability p.
intro: Mathematics provides universal tools for understanding real-world outcomes: algebra, geometry, calculus, statistics, and probability theory. Whether you're a student exploring binomial distributions or a professional validating theoretical models, this free calculator computes P(X=k) for n independent trials with constant probability p. Results are updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you current.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **P = C(n,k)·p^k·(1-p)^(n-k)**. Enter the number of trials (n), number of successes (k), and probability of success (p) to instantly calculate P(X=k), mean, and variance.

example.title: Example calculation
steps: 10 coin flips, 3 heads, probability 0.5 per flip

explanation:
**Mathematics** is a universal tool for modeling real-world phenomena. Whether in engineering, science, finance, or everyday life, binomial probability helps answer questions about repeated trials with fixed outcomes.

## How it works

Binomial probability calculates the likelihood of exactly k successes in n independent trials, each with probability p. This calculator applies the standard formula with your values and alerts you if inputs fall outside typical ranges.

## Key concepts

- **n (trials)**: Total number of independent experiments
- **k (successes)**: Number of desired positive outcomes
- **p (probability)**: Likelihood of success on each single trial
- **P(X=k)**: Probability of exactly k successes in n trials

## Important note

This calculator is a reference tool. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: When do I use binomial probability?
faq.a: When you have a fixed number of independent trials (n), two possible outcomes per trial (success/failure), and constant probability (p) on each trial.

faq.q: What's the difference between binomial and normal distribution?
faq.a: Binomial applies to discrete trials with two outcomes. Normal distribution is continuous. You can approximate binomial with normal when both np ≥ 5 and n(1-p) ≥ 5.

faq.q: Can I calculate cumulative probability P(X ≤ k)?
faq.a: This calculator shows P(X=k). Cumulative probability sums all probabilities from 0 to k. You can calculate it by running multiple queries and adding results.
It's the likelihood of your desired outcome (heads in a coin flip, correct answer on a test question) on any single trial. Must be between 0 and 1.
Flipping 10 coins and calculating the probability of exactly 3 heads: n=10, k=3, p=0.5 gives 11.7%.
Yes. We use internationally validated formulas. For critical decisions, verify with a statistician or specialist.

Bernoulli is a special case of binomial when n=1 (single trial with two outcomes).
